title: Model card and human oversight

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Model card and human oversight; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A model card links intended use, exclusions, users, data, training/evaluation procedure, metrics and slices, limitations, ethical/risk considerations, monitoring, human oversight, and escalation to recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Every performance claim cites a versioned evaluation table and date, while known unsupported languages and prohibited high-stakes uses appear prominently. Your goal is to write a model card whose claims match recorded evaluation evidence. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Evaluation and responsible AI; Language, perception and embodied applications; Reproducible pipeline; Fairness and explainability. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: A model card links intended use, exclusions, users, data, training/evaluation procedure, metrics and slices, limitations, ethical/risk considerations, monitoring, human oversight, and escalation to recorded evidence. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Documentation can become stale, aggregate metrics hide failures, intended use does not prevent misuse, proprietary gaps reduce review, human oversight needs authority and capacity, and a card is not certification. The learner must reject this overclaim: Publishing a model card makes a model transparent, fair, and safe even when evidence is incomplete or deployment changes.

includes: Report intended use, exclusions, data, metrics, slices, limitations, review points and escalation paths.
Write a model card whose claims match recorded evaluation evidence.
excludes: Documentation can become stale, aggregate metrics hide failures, intended use does not prevent misuse, proprietary gaps reduce review, human oversight needs authority and capacity, and a card is not certification.

outcomes: Write a model card whose claims match recorded evaluation evidence.

examples.title: Model card and human oversight: bounded direct case
examples.situation: Every performance claim cites a versioned evaluation table and date, while known unsupported languages and prohibited high-stakes uses appear prominently.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: Every performance claim cites a versioned evaluation table and date, while known unsupported languages and prohibited high-stakes uses appear prominently.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Keep model-card claims traceable to versioned evidence, name exclusions and uncertainty, and connect oversight, monitoring, escalation, and update ownership.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Keep model-card claims traceable to versioned evidence, name exclusions and uncertainty, and connect oversight, monitoring, escalation, and update ownership.

examples.title: Model card and human oversight: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: A new deployment population falls outside evaluated scope, so the card blocks reuse pending mapping and testing instead of inheriting old claims.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: A new deployment population falls outside evaluated scope, so the card blocks reuse pending mapping and testing instead of inheriting old claims.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Documentation can become stale, aggregate metrics hide failures, intended use does not prevent misuse, proprietary gaps reduce review, human oversight needs authority and capacity, and a card is not certification.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: Publishing a model card makes a model transparent, fair, and safe even when evidence is incomplete or deployment changes.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Write a model card whose claims match recorded evaluation evidence.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Publishing a model card makes a model transparent, fair, and safe even when evidence is incomplete or deployment changes.
misconceptions.correction: Keep model-card claims traceable to versioned evidence, name exclusions and uncertainty, and connect oversight, monitoring, escalation, and update ownership.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: Publishing a model card makes a model transparent, fair, and safe even when evidence is incomplete or deployment changes.

analogy.example: a reviewable experiment package containing scope, baseline, data/version manifests, evaluation, model card, risk register, and reproduction commands can introduce Model card and human oversight only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: project design, reproducibility, oversight documentation, and capstone defense
limitations: documentation and a polished demo are bounded evidence, not production authorization or proof of safe capability

recap.summary: You should now be able to write a model card whose claims match recorded evaluation evidence. Keep this boundary in view: Documentation can become stale, aggregate metrics hide failures, intended use does not prevent misuse, proprietary gaps reduce review, human oversight needs authority and capacity, and a card is not certification.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Model card and human oversight with one technical mechanism, one evidence requirement, and one non-claim.
